199	- **Do NOT use undefined macros.** Every `\foo` your prose needs must exist in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` or be `\providecommand`'d at the top of your chapter (chapter-local is fine; report a "needs macro" note for shared ones).
200	- **Do NOT touch protected blocks.** Your invocation prompt's "Protected by the mathematician" section lists `archon-protected.yaml` rules. A `statement`-protected label's declaration block (statement + `\label`/`\lean`/`\uses`) is frozen — you may still write its `\begin{proof}`; an `all`-protected label is entirely off-limits. You may freely `\uses{}` protected labels from other blocks. If your directive conflicts with a protection rule, stop and report the conflict instead of editing.
201	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
202	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
203	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
204	
205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
277	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
278	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
279	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
280	
281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
294	
295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.
297	- **Removal** (directive-authorized only): delete the orphaned block cleanly and record it in your report. Never remove the goal or a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
298	
299	Keep `leandag` edits inside your assigned chapter — fixing a dependency that lives in another chapter is a "Notes for Plan Agent" item, not a cross-chapter edit.
300

The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.

29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \

42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.

46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:

91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.

11	% (Lemmas \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial},
12	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero},
13	% \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent},

17	\section{Setup and motivation}
18	
19	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
20	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
21	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
22	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
23	derived functor via injective resolutions, which requires the ambient category of
24	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives. The {\v C}ech approach
25	developed here sidesteps this requirement:
26	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
27	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
28	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
29	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
30	
31	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
32	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
33	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
34	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
35	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
36	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
37	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
38	\[
39	  \begin{array}{ccc}
40	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
41	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
42	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
43	  \end{array}
44	\]
45	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
46	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
47	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
48	
49	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
50	
51	\begin{definition}
52	
53	\leanok
54	[{\v C}ech nerve of an affine open cover]
55	  \label{def:cech_nerve}
56	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve}
57	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, def:push_pull_functor, def:cech_nerve_cosimplicial, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
58	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
59	  % quasi-coherent sheaves, opening paragraph and Lemma
60	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}
61	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L36--67).
62	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
63	  % Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
64	  % Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
65	  % of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
66	  % where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
67	  % the unit ideal, see
68	  % Schemes, Definition \ref{schemes-definition-standard-covering}."
69	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
70	  quasi-coherent sheaves.}
71	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
72	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite open cover of \(X\) by
73	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
74	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write
75	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = U_{i_0} \cap \cdots \cap U_{i_p}\) for the corresponding
76	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
77	  \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in
78	  cosimplicial degree \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
79	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
80	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
81	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
82	  \[
83	    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
84	      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
85	        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
86	  \]
87	  with cofaces given by the restriction maps that omit one index and
88	  codegeneracies by the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree
89	  \(-1\) is the object on all of \(X\). The variance is essential: the geometric
90	  nerve of the cover (the iterated fibre powers of \(\coprod_i U_i\) over \(X\),
91	  introduced in \S\ref{sec:cech_three_part} below) is a \emph{simplicial} object
92	  in \emph{schemes}, but applying the covariant direct-image functor turns it into
93	  a \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); consequently the
94	  associated alternating-coface complex is a \emph{cochain} complex (differentials
95	  raising degree), not a chain complex. When \(X\) is separated each intersection
96	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
97	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
98	  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
99	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
100	  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).
101	\end{definition}
102	
103	\begin{definition}
104	
105	\leanok
106	[{\v C}ech complex of a quasi-coherent sheaf]
107	  \label{def:cech_complex}
108	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechComplex}
109	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
110	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
111	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof)
112	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L59--77).
113	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
114	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
115	  % is identified with the complex
116	  % $$
117	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
118	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
119	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
120	  % \ldots
121	  % $$
122	  % We are asked to show that the extended complex
123	  % \begin{equation}
124	  % \label{equation-extended}
125	  % 0 \to
126	  % M \to
127	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to

129	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to

132	  % (whose truncation we have studied in
133	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact."
134	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
135	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover {\v C}ech
136	  vanishing).}
137	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
138	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with
139	  all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) affine (e.g.\ \(X\) separated). The
140	  \emph{{\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
141	  is the cochain complex obtained by applying the global-sections functor to the
142	  {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\);
143	  in degree \(p\) it is
144	  \[
145	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
146	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
147	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
148	  \]
149	  with differential \(d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^{p+1}\) the

153	  \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) and a standard cover by the
154	  \(D(f_i)\), this is the complex
155	  \(\prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \to \cdots\) of
156	  localisations. The \emph{relative {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet
157	  (\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) for \(f : X \to S\) is the analogous complex in
158	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) obtained by applying \(f_*\) over each finite

162	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) because each \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine and the
163	  pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated

165	\end{definition}

167	\subsection{The three-part construction of the relative {\v C}ech complex}
168	\label{sec:cech_three_part}
169	
170	It is convenient to factor the construction of
171	Definitions~\ref{def:cech_nerve}--\ref{def:cech_complex} into three independent
172	pieces. Two of them are purely formal and unconditional; the sole non-trivial
173	mathematical content is isolated in the middle piece.
174	
175	\paragraph{(1) Geometric backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow.}
176	Package the affine cover as a single morphism
177	\(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) (the canonical map out of the
178	coproduct induced by the inclusions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)). The augmented
179	{\v C}ech nerve of this arrow is an augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
180	\(X\): its object in simplicial degree \(p\) is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
181	\[
182	  \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
183	    \;=\;
184	    \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
185	      U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
186	\]
187	with the augmentation the cover map to \(X\). Because the \(U_i\) are open
188	subschemes of \(X\), each fibre product \(U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p}\)
189	is canonically the intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). This backbone exists
190	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts
191	and finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the {\v C}ech nerve of an
192	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of
193	spaces over which the construction is indexed.
194	
195	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
196	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by
197	the \emph{relative-direct-image functor on the over-category}
198	\[
199	  G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
200	  \qquad
201	  (Y \xrightarrow{\,p\,} X) \;\longmapsto\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
202	\]
203	sending an \(X\)-scheme \(p : Y \to X\) to the pushforward along \(p\) of the
204	pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\). Composing \(G\) with the (opposite of the) simplicial
205	backbone of (1) produces exactly the augmented cosimplicial object of
206	Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: on the degree-\(p\) piece
207	\(p : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\) one has
208	\(p_* p^*\mathcal{F} = (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\),
209	and a morphism of \(X\)-schemes \(h : (Y_1, p_1) \to (Y_2, p_2)\) (so
210	\(h \circ p_2 = p_1\)\,, reading \(p\) as the structure map) induces the
211	restriction comparison \(G(p_2) \to G(p_1)\) via the unit/counit of the
212	pull-push adjunction together with the over-triangle identifying the two
213	structure maps. This functor \(G\) is the \emph{one non-formal step} in the whole
214	construction. Its functoriality --- that \(G\) respects identities and composition
215	of morphisms over \(X\) --- is precisely a statement about the coherence of the
216	comparison isomorphisms \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) and
217	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) for pushforward and pullback of sheaves of
218	modules, together with their compatibility with the adjunction units. Concretely,
219	pullback and pushforward of sheaves of modules are organised as a
220	pseudofunctor \(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to
221	\mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\) (sending a morphism to the adjoint pair
222	\(p^* \dashv p_*\)), and the comparison isomorphisms
223	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\), \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) are its structure
224	\(2\)-cells. The functoriality of \(G\) needs exactly three facts: the mate identity
225	expressing the conjugate of the inverse pullback comparison as the pushforward
226	comparison, its normalisation at the identity morphism, and the pseudofunctor
227	unitor and pentagon (associativity) coherences. The laws are nonetheless not formal
228	trivialities: each is a multi-step mate calculation that transports the comparison
229	isomorphisms along the over-triangle \(g \circ p_2 = p_1\) of structure maps and
230	past the adjunction unit, transporting along the over-triangle equality; the
231	obstacle is the bookkeeping of those transports, not a missing geometric ingredient.
232	
233	\paragraph{(3) Coherence-free plumbing — from the nerve to the cochain complex.}
234	The passage from the augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module of (1)--(2)
235	to the relative {\v C}ech complex in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) is again purely
236	formal and uses \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence. One forgets the
237	augmentation, pushes the cosimplicial object forward along \(f : X \to S\) (a
238	covariant operation applied degreewise), and takes the alternating-coface-map
239	cochain complex of the resulting cosimplicial object of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules.
240	This step relies only on the preadditivity of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\): the
241	alternating sum \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j d^j\) of the cofaces is defined in any
242	preadditive category and squares to zero by the cosimplicial identities. Thus the
243	relative {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
244	of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is genuinely \emph{defined} in terms of the
245	{\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: once the nerve is constructed,
246	the complex follows with no further input. In summary, of the three pieces only
247	the push--pull functor (2) requires content beyond formal category theory, and
248	that content is the pseudofunctor coherence of pushforward and pullback: the
249	comparison \(2\)-cells of the pseudofunctor
250	\(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to \mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\)
251	and their unitor/pentagon identities.
252	
253	\paragraph{Formal bricks of the three-part construction.}
254	The geometric backbone (1), the push--pull functor (2), and the plumbing (3) are
255	recorded as the following declarations. The backbone and plumbing are
256	unconditional; the push--pull object and morphism maps are likewise unconditional
257	bricks, and the functor laws assembling them are the lone coherence statement.

301	  \label{def:push_pull_map}
302	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_eq_raw, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self_gen, AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardComp_hom_app_id, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_comp}
303	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj}
304	  The morphism map of \(G\). A morphism \(g : (Y_2, p_2) \to (Y_1, p_1)\) of
305	  \(X\)-schemes has underlying map \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) satisfying the
306	  over-triangle \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\); the contravariant functor produces the
307	  restriction comparison \(G(Y_1, p_1) \to G(Y_2, p_2)\) as the five-step composite
308	  \[
309	    p_{1*}\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
310	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{1*}(\eta)\,}
311	    p_{1*}\,\bar g_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
312	      \xrightarrow{\,\sim\,}
313	    (p_1 \circ \bar g)_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
314	      \;=\;
315	    p_{2*}\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
316	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{2*}(\sim)\,}
317	    p_{2*}\,p_2^*\mathcal{F},

321	  the equality is the substitution \(p_2 = p_1 \circ \bar g\) of the over-triangle,
322	  and the last arrow applies \(p_{2*}\) to the pullback comparison
323	  \(\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (p_1 \bar g)^* = p_2^*\). The two over-triangle
324	  substitutions are realised as coercions along \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\).

375	  \label{lem:push_pull_unit_mate}

514	\mathcal{F})\) of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} --- a cochain complex of
515	abelian groups --- whereas the combinatorial contracting-homotopy core operates on a

518	section identification (each section group is an away localisation, with the

523	Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} below; the assembly --- feeding each
524	positive-degree node through the local-to-global exactness criterion and closing with
525	the dependent combinatorial port --- is carried out in the proof of
526	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}.

2791	dimension-shift proof needs three structural facts about \(H^p(U, -)\): a degree-zero
2792	identification with global sections, vanishing on injective modules, and a long exact
2793	sequence attached to a short exact sequence of modules. We realize \(H^p(U, -)\) as the
2794	Ext of the structure sheaf, where Mathlib supplies all three facts off the shelf; the
2795	following definition fixes that realization, and the Mathlib dependency anchors after it
2796	pin the exact results it rests on.

2798	\begin{definition}
2799	[Absolute sheaf cohomology as Ext of the structure sheaf]
2800	  \label{def:absolute_cohomology}
2801	  \uses{lem:ext_bifunctor_mathlib, lem:ext_covariant_les_mathlib,
2802	    lem:ext_eq_zero_of_injective_mathlib, lem:ext_homequiv_zero_mathlib,
2803	    lem:hasext_standard_mathlib, lem:modules_restrict_functor_mathlib}
2804	  \textit{Realization of absolute sheaf cohomology in the module-sheaf category.}
2805	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(U \subseteq
2806	  X\) an open subscheme with open immersion \(j : U \hookrightarrow X\). Write
2807	  \(\mathcal{F}|_U\) for the restriction of \(\mathcal{F}\) along \(j\) — the image of
2808	  \(\mathcal{F}\) under the Mathlib restriction functor \(j^\ast :
2809	  X.\mathrm{Modules} \to U.\mathrm{Modules}\) of
2810	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_restrict_functor_mathlib} — and \(\mathcal{O}_U\) for the
2811	  structure sheaf of \(U\) regarded as a module over itself. The \emph{absolute sheaf
2812	  cohomology of \(\mathcal{F}\) over \(U\)} is defined to be the Ext of the structure
2813	  sheaf in the abelian category \(U.\mathrm{Modules} = \mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_U)\) of
2814	  sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-modules:
2815	  \[
2816	    H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) \;:=\; \operatorname{Ext}^p_{\mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_U)}
2817	      \bigl(\mathcal{O}_U,\; \mathcal{F}|_U\bigr),
2818	    \qquad p \geq 0,
2819	  \]
2820	  equivalently \(\operatorname{Ext}^p_{\mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}(j_!\mathcal{O}_U,
2821	  \mathcal{F})\). Here \(\operatorname{Ext}\) is Mathlib's Ext bifunctor
2822	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_bifunctor_mathlib}) on the abelian category
2823	  \(U.\mathrm{Modules}\); the \(\mathrm{HasExt}\) structure it requires is available
2824	  unconditionally by Lemma~\ref{lem:hasext_standard_mathlib}. The three structural facts
2825	  the downstream dimension-shift arguments consume are then exactly the corresponding
2826	  Mathlib results:
2827	  \begin{enumerate}
2828	  \item \textbf{Degree zero is global sections.} In degree \(0\), Ext is the Hom-group,
2829	    \(\operatorname{Ext}^0(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{F}|_U) \cong
2830	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_U}(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{F}|_U) = \Gamma(U,
2831	    \mathcal{F})\), by the degree-zero Ext comparison
2832	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_homequiv_zero_mathlib}); so \(H^0(U, \mathcal{F}) \cong
2833	    \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\).
2834	  \item \textbf{Injective vanishing.} When the \emph{second} Ext argument is an
2835	    injective module \(\mathcal{J}\), one has \(\operatorname{Ext}^{n+1}(-,
2836	    \mathcal{J}) = 0\) by Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_eq_zero_of_injective_mathlib}. Placing an
2837	    injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{I}\) in that second-argument role
2838	    (via the \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) form over \(\mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\)) yields
2839	    \(H^{n+1}(U, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) directly, with no appeal to restriction preserving
2840	    injectives.
2841	  \item \textbf{Long exact sequence.} For a short exact sequence \(0 \to \mathcal{F}
2842	    \to \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0\) of modules and the \emph{fixed} first
2843	    argument \(\mathcal{O}_U\), the covariant Ext long exact sequence
2844	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_covariant_les_mathlib}) is the long exact sequence of the
2845	    groups \(H^p(U, -)\), with connecting maps given by composition with the Ext class
2846	    of the extension.
2847	  \end{enumerate}
2848	\end{definition}

\section{Absolute sheaf cohomology as Ext of the corepresenting object}

The two vanishing lemmas below speak about the \emph{absolute} sheaf cohomology
\(H^p(U, \mathcal{F})\) of an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module over an open \(U\). Their
dimension-shift proof needs three structural facts about \(H^p(U, -)\): a degree-zero
identification with global sections, vanishing on injective modules, and a long exact
sequence attached to a short exact sequence of modules. We realize \(H^p(U, -)\) as the
\emph{Ext of a corepresenting object} in the single abelian category
\(X.\mathrm{Modules} = \mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\): there is an
\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) corepresenting the
sections-over-\(U\) functor \(\mathcal{F} \mapsto \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\), and we set
\(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = \operatorname{Ext}^p_{\mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}(j_!\mathcal{O}_U,
\mathcal{F})\). The corepresenting object is built \emph{without} the extension-by-zero
functor — it is the sheafification of the free module on the representable presheaf
\(\mathbf{y}\,U\) — and the realization keeps the injective module in the \emph{second}
Ext argument, where Mathlib supplies all three facts off the shelf with \emph{no} appeal
to restriction of module sheaves. The corepresenting object and its corepresentability
are fixed first, then the following definition fixes the realization, and the Mathlib
dependency anchors after it pin the exact results it rests on.

\begin{definition}
[Extension-by-zero structure sheaf as a corepresenting object]
  \label{def:jshriek_ou}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.jShriekOU}
  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, lem:mod_pmod_adjunction,
    lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}
  \textit{Corepresenting object for sections over an open, built without the
  extension-by-zero functor.}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an open subscheme, regarded through the
  representable presheaf \(\mathbf{y}\,U\) on the site of opens of \(X\). The
  \emph{extension-by-zero structure sheaf} \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) is the object of
  \(X.\mathrm{Modules} = \mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) obtained by sheafifying the free
  presheaf of modules on \(\mathbf{y}\,U\):
  \[
    j_!\mathcal{O}_U \;:=\; \operatorname{sheafification}\bigl(
      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U)\bigr)
    \;\in\; X.\mathrm{Modules},
  \]
  where \(\operatorname{free}\) is the free presheaf-of-modules functor on the
  representable (the degree-zero generator of the free presheaf complex of
  Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}) and
  \(\operatorname{sheafification} \dashv \iota\) is the sheafification adjunction of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}. Up to isomorphism this is the classical
  extension-by-zero \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) of the structure sheaf of \(U\) along the open
  immersion \(j : U \hookrightarrow X\); crucially, we build only this \emph{object} as a
  sheafified free module, never the extension-by-zero \emph{functor} \(j_!\). The object
  exists unconditionally: the free presheaf of modules on a representable and the
  sheafification of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules are both supplied by Mathlib.
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}
[Corepresentability of global sections over \(U\)]
  \label{lem:jshriek_corepr}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.jShriekOU_homEquiv}
  \uses{def:jshriek_ou, lem:mod_pmod_adjunction, lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}
  \textit{The object \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) corepresents the sections-over-\(U\) functor.}
  For every \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) there is a natural additive
  isomorphism
  \[
    \operatorname{Hom}_{X.\mathrm{Modules}}\bigl(j_!\mathcal{O}_U,\, \mathcal{F}\bigr)
    \;\cong\;
    \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F}) \;=\; \mathcal{F}(U),
  \]
  natural in \(\mathcal{F}\). Thus \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) corepresents the global-sections
  functor \(\mathcal{F} \mapsto \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\) on \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:jshriek_ou, lem:mod_pmod_adjunction, lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}
  Compose two natural additive isomorphisms. The sheafification adjunction
  \(\operatorname{sheafification} \dashv \iota\) of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction} transports corepresentability through
  sheafification:
  \[
    \operatorname{Hom}_{X.\mathrm{Modules}}\bigl(
      \operatorname{sheafification}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U)),\, \mathcal{F}
    \bigr)
    \;\cong\;
    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}\bigl(
      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U),\, \iota\mathcal{F}\bigr).
  \]
  The free–Yoneda additive equivalence \(\operatorname{freeYonedaHomAddEquiv}\) of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification} then identifies the right-hand
  presheaf Hom-group with the evaluation \((\iota\mathcal{F})(U) = \mathcal{F}(U) =
  \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\):
  \[
    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}\bigl(
      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U),\, \iota\mathcal{F}\bigr)
    \;\cong\;
    \mathcal{F}(U).
  \]
  Both isomorphisms are additive and natural in \(\mathcal{F}\), so their composite is
  the asserted natural additive isomorphism.
\end{proof}

\begin{definition}
[Absolute sheaf cohomology as Ext of the structure sheaf]
  \label{def:absolute_cohomology}
  \uses{lem:ext_bifunctor_mathlib, lem:ext_covariant_les_mathlib,
    lem:ext_eq_zero_of_injective_mathlib, lem:ext_homequiv_zero_mathlib,
    lem:hasext_standard_mathlib, lem:modules_restrict_functor_mathlib}
  \textit{Realization of absolute sheaf cohomology in the module-sheaf category.}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(U \subseteq
  X\) an open subscheme with open immersion \(j : U \hookrightarrow X\). Write
  \(\mathcal{F}|_U\) for the restriction of \(\mathcal{F}\) along \(j\) — the image of
  \(\mathcal{F}\) under the Mathlib restriction functor \(j^\ast :
  X.\mathrm{Modules} \to U.\mathrm{Modules}\) of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_restrict_functor_mathlib} — and \(\mathcal{O}_U\) for the
  structure sheaf of \(U\) regarded as a module over itself. The \emph{absolute sheaf
  cohomology of \(\mathcal{F}\) over \(U\)} is defined to be the Ext of the structure
  sheaf in the abelian category \(U.\mathrm{Modules} = \mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_U)\) of
  sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-modules:
  \[
    H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) \;:=\; \operatorname{Ext}^p_{\mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_U)}
      \bigl(\mathcal{O}_U,\; \mathcal{F}|_U\bigr),
    \qquad p \geq 0,
  \]
  equivalently \(\operatorname{Ext}^p_{\mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}(j_!\mathcal{O}_U,
  \mathcal{F})\). Here \(\operatorname{Ext}\) is Mathlib's Ext bifunctor
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_bifunctor_mathlib}) on the abelian category
  \(U.\mathrm{Modules}\); the \(\mathrm{HasExt}\) structure it requires is available
  unconditionally by Lemma~\ref{lem:hasext_standard_mathlib}. The three structural facts
  the downstream dimension-shift arguments consume are then exactly the corresponding
  Mathlib results:
  \begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Degree zero is global sections.} In degree \(0\), Ext is the Hom-group,
    \(\operatorname{Ext}^0(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{F}|_U) \cong
    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_U}(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{F}|_U) = \Gamma(U,
    \mathcal{F})\), by the degree-zero Ext comparison
    (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_homequiv_zero_mathlib}); so \(H^0(U, \mathcal{F}) \cong
    \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\).
  \item \textbf{Injective vanishing.} When the \emph{second} Ext argument is an
    injective module \(\mathcal{J}\), one has \(\operatorname{Ext}^{n+1}(-,
    \mathcal{J}) = 0\) by Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_eq_zero_of_injective_mathlib}. Placing an
    injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{I}\) in that second-argument role
    (via the \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) form over \(\mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\)) yields
    \(H^{n+1}(U, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) directly, with no appeal to restriction preserving
    injectives.
  \item \textbf{Long exact sequence.} For a short exact sequence \(0 \to \mathcal{F}
    \to \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0\) of modules and the \emph{fixed} first
    argument \(\mathcal{O}_U\), the covariant Ext long exact sequence
    (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_covariant_les_mathlib}) is the long exact sequence of the
    groups \(H^p(U, -)\), with connecting maps given by composition with the Ext class
    of the extension.
  \end{enumerate}
\end{definition}
